title: 6.C35 A2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
description: In this assignment, I performed an exploratory data analysis of the MBTA system to answer questions about the coverage and utility of the system.
story: 6.C35 tasked us with exploring a dataset as thoroughly as possible, answering questions we had and uncovering new questions to answer based on interesting patterns in the dataset. This assignment also helped us prepare for the final project, and the dataset we chose corresponded to a specific aspect of housing reform in Boston. The MBTA dataset that I used was tailored towards Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) planning, and I used it to answer questions about the coverage and utility of the system.

title: 6.C35 A3 - Visualization Critique & Redesign
description: In this assignment, I critiqued and redesigned a visualization to be more effective.
story: 6.C35 tasked us with critiquing and redesigning a popular spiral-shaped visualization of COVID-19 cases over time to be more effective. We analyzed the benefits of various design choices, such as architectural structure, color encodings, annotations, and more. My attempt at a revised graphic aimed to increase the emotional impact of the visualization and increasing the amount of context provided.

title: 6.C35 A4 - Persuasive or Deceptive Visualization?
description: In this assignment, I designed two visualizations to support one of each side of an argument.
story: 6.C35 tasked us with designing two visualizations around a specific dataset--in my case, I chose the IMF climate change dataset. I chose to design a visualization that supported the proposition that China leads the world in fighting climate change, and a visualization that supported the proposition that China does not lead the world in fighting climate change. I designed the first visualization to show China's emissions per capita, emissions targets per capita, and renewable & low-carbon tech investment compared to other countries. I designed the second visualization to show China's emissions, emissions targets, and fossil fuel subsidies compared to other countries.

title: UROP - Time Series Foundation Models for Variable Star Classification
description: In this project, I fine-tuned several popular time series foundation models to classify variable star light curves, and compared their performance to a custom-built model.
story: As part of my UROP at the Kavli Institute for Astrophysics and Space Research, I trained several time series foundation models developed by large companies like Google and IBM to classify variable star light curves. These models were able to transfer some of their knowledge of time series to the light curves, and showed higher performance for smaller dataset sizes, but given enough training data, a custom-built model was able to achieve higher performance relative to model size.

title: UROP - Diffusion Autoencoder with Perceivers (daep) for Spectra Reconstruction
description: In this project, I trained Y. Shen and A. Gagliano's diffusion autoencoder perceiver (their paper is linked here) to reconstruct spectra as a self-supervised pretraining task.
story: As part of my UROP at the Kavli Institute for Astrophysics and Space Research, I tested several machine learning models' ability to learn the structure of spectra from the GALAH survey, a large-scale survey of stars in the Milky Way. The best of these models was Yunyi Shen and Alex Gagliano's diffusion autoencoder perceiver (daep), which was able to reconstruct spectra from the GALAH survey with much more precision than even some models specialized to spectra.

title: 6.S058 - MRI Scan Kidney Segmentation
description: In this project, my partner and I developed a U-Net based model to segment 3D structures of the kidney from MRI scans of mice.
story: My computer science class (6.S058) involved combining the 3-dimensional images taken by MRI scanners and identifying the kidneys, a task necessary for research taking place at the Lemaire Lab at the HOspital for Sick Children in Toronto. Image segmentation is a common computer vision task, but working with 3D images provides much more context to be used. Unfortunately, the dataset available for this task was small, so we experimented with different data augmentation techniques to improve the performance of our model.

title: Akamai Internship - Photometric Processing Pipeline
description: In this project, I developed an image processing pipeline to perform basic reduction, astrometry, and photometry on images from the Nickel Telescope.
story: In the summer of 2024, I interned at the UC Observatories via the Akamai Workforce Initiative. One of the telescopes in the UC system is the Nickel Telescope, which is a smaller, 40-in reflector telescope located just outside San Jose, at about 4,200 ft. This telescope is often used to broaden telescope access to those with less astornomical observation experience, so I was tasked with smoothing this experience by developing a pipeline to perform basic reduction, astrometry, and photometry on images from the telescope.

title: 6.C35 Lab 9 - Geospatial Visualizations
description: In this lab, I built an interactive map of Boston bike traffic using Mapbox and D3, with features like bike lane overlays, station demand markers, time-based filtering, and isochrone visualizations.
story: 6.C35 Lab 9 tasked us with building an interactive geospatial visualization of Boston bike traffic. Using Mapbox for mapping and D3 for data overlays, I built a map featuring bike lane displays, station markers sized by demand, time-based filtering, and isochrone visualization to show biking accessibility across the city.
